name: PIWI-piRNA pathway regulation
description: A regulatory mechanism in which the PIWI-piRNA pathway controls the activity of specific transposons, such as L1Hs, in human embryonic stem cells.

name: PKR inhibition
description: PKR inhibition is the suppression of the activity of the double-stranded RNA-activated protein kinase (PKR).

name: POU1F1-dependent activation
description: POU1F1-dependent activation is the process by which the transcription factor POU1F1 (Pit-1) stimulates gene expression.

name: Pachytene cluster piRNA targeting
description: A mode of piRNA production at postnatal stages where piRNAs from pachytene clusters target older TEs.

name: Paired-ends complex assembly
description: Paired-ends complex assembly is a step in transposition where the transposase brings the two ends of the transposon together.

name: Parallel evolution
description: Parallel evolution refers to the independent emergence of similar non-canonical SVA structures in chimpanzees and humans.

name: Partial inclusion in mRNA
description: Partial inclusion in mRNA refers to the aberrant splicing event where part of a transposon sequence is retained in the mature messenger RNA transcript.

name: Partial loss of gene function
description: Partial loss of gene function refers to a reduction, but not complete absence, of a gene's normal activity.

name: Participating in evolutionary processes
description: Participating in evolutionary processes refers to the role of genetic elements in driving or contributing to genomic changes over evolutionary time.

name: Passive co-transcription
description: Passive co-transcription refers to the inclusion of LINE-1 sequences in transcripts originating from other genomic loci.

name: Paternal bias in retrotransposition
description: Paternal bias in retrotransposition refers to the phenomenon where new retrotransposition events originate more frequently from the paternal germline.

name: Pathogenesis
description: Pathogenesis refers to the process by which retrotransposon activity can lead to disease.

name: Pathological processes of neurological diseases
description: Pathological processes refer to the molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying the development and progression of neurological disorders.

name: Pb(II) binding
description: Pb(II) binding refers to the interaction between lead ions and specific protein domains, such as the tris-cysteine layers in the coiled-coil domain of ORF1p.

name: Peptide generation
description: The production of protein fragments or peptides derived from sequences within RNA transcripts.

name: Pericentric inversion
description: A pericentric inversion is a chromosomal rearrangement where a segment of a chromosome, containing the centromere, is reversed end to end.

name: Persistent DNA damage
description: Persistent DNA damage refers to DNA lesions, such as double-strand breaks, that are not efficiently repaired and accumulate over time.

name: Phase separation
description: Phase separation is a biophysical process where biomolecules condense into distinct liquid-like compartments, as demonstrated for the ORF1 protein of LINE-1.

name: Phosphorylation site homology
description: The presence of similar amino acid sequences that can be phosphorylated by the same kinase in different proteins.

name: Photoaging
description: Photoaging is the premature aging of the skin caused by repeated exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation.

name: Phylogenetic analysis
description: Phylogenetic analysis is a method used to infer evolutionary relationships among biological entities based on sequence data.

name: Phylogenetic marker
description: A phylogenetic marker is a genetic character used to infer evolutionary relationships among species or lineages.

name: Physical mapping
description: Physical mapping is a technique used to determine the physical location of DNA sequences on chromosomes.

name: Pituitary GH gene regulation
description: Pituitary GH gene regulation refers to the molecular mechanisms controlling the expression of the growth hormone gene in the pituitary gland.

name: Piwi pathway
description: The Piwi pathway is a mechanism involving Piwi proteins and Piwi-interacting RNAs (piRNAs) that silences transposable elements in germ cells.

name: Placental CRH expression
description: The expression of corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) in the placenta, specifically in placental syncytiotrophoblasts.

name: Placental expression regulation
description: The process by which the expression of genes, specifically the ERVWE1 locus encoding syncytin, is controlled in placental tissue.

name: Placental physiology
description: Placental physiology refers to the biological processes and functions of the placenta.

name: Placentation
description: Placentation is the process of forming the placenta, which is essential for pregnancy.

name: Pluripotency
description: Pluripotency is the state of a stem cell that has the potential to differentiate into any of the three germ layers.

name: Pluripotency factor expression
description: Pluripotency factor expression refers to the transcriptional activity of genes like OCT4, SOX2, and NANOG, which maintain the pluripotent state of cells.

name: Pol II transcription
description: Transcription by RNA polymerase II, which transcribes Alu RNAs embedded within coding or noncoding RNA transcripts.

name: Pol II transcription complex assembly
description: The assembly of RNA polymerase II and associated factors at a promoter to initiate transcription.

name: Pol II-binding aptamer (RAP) activity
description: Pol II-binding aptamer (RAP) activity refers to the ability of specific RNA sequences to bind RNA polymerase II.

name: Pol III transcription
description: Transcription by RNA polymerase III, which transcribes free Alu RNAs.

name: Poly(A) binding protein (PABP) interaction
description: The interaction between the A-rich region of SINE RNAs and the poly(A) binding protein (PABP).

name: Poly(A) microsatellite formation
description: Poly(A) microsatellite formation refers to the concurrent creation of a poly(A) DNA tract at the 3'-end of a retrotransposon insertion, which serves as the origin for a new microsatellite.

name: Poly(A) tail dynamics
description: Poly(A) tail dynamics refer to the processes regulating the length and integrity of the polyadenylate tail at the 3' end of retrotransposon transcripts, which can influence their stability and retrotransposition efficiency.

name: Poly(A) tail length-dependent mobility
description: The rate of Alu retrotransposition varies significantly depending on the length of its poly(A) tail.

name: Polyadenylation
description: Polyadenylation is the addition of a poly(A) tail to the 3' end of an RNA transcript, often involved in its maturation and stability.

name: Polyadenylation at the 3' end
description: A process involving conserved cis-acting elements (hexamer, cleavage site, GU-rich region) for adding a polyA tail to the 3' end of LINE-1 elements.

name: Polyadenylation bypass
description: Polyadenylation bypass is a process where L1 mRNAs sometimes bypass their own polyadenylation signal and use fortuitous signals in flanking genomic DNA.

name: Polyadenylation function
description: Polyadenylation function refers to the ability of a DNA sequence, such as an LTR, to signal the addition of a poly(A) tail to an RNA transcript.

name: Polymerization of ORF1p
description: The process by which human L1 ORF1p forms polymers under low-salt conditions in the absence of nucleic acids.

name: Polymorphic SVA insertion
description: Polymorphic SVA insertion refers to SVA insertions that vary between individuals, contributing to genomic variation.

name: Polymorphic TE insertion
description: Polymorphic TE insertion refers to a transposable element insertion that is present in some individuals and absent in others, creating genetic variation.

name: Polymorphic transposable element insertion
description: Polymorphic transposable element insertion refers to the presence of a transposon at a specific genomic locus that varies among individuals in a population.

name: Polymorphism
description: Polymorphism refers to the presence of multiple sequence variants at a specific genomic locus in a population.

name: Population evolution analysis
description: Population evolution analysis is the study of genetic variation and changes in allele frequencies within and between populations over time.

name: Population genetic marker
description: A population genetic marker is a genetic variant used to study the genetic structure, history, and diversity of populations.

name: Population subdivision
description: Population subdivision refers to the division of a large population into smaller, reproductively isolated subpopulations (demes).

name: Pose risk to genome integrity
description: The potential of retrotransposons to cause harmful genomic instability through their activity.

name: Positive feedback loop
description: A positive feedback loop is a state where increased LINE-1 activity leads to exponential growth in cDNA copy number, as predicted by the computational model.

name: Positive selection
description: Positive selection is a form of natural selection that increases the frequency of a beneficial allele in a population.

name: Post-insertion selection
description: The process of natural selection acting on transposon copies after their integration into the genome.

name: Post-insertional genomic injury
description: Genomic damage occurring after the insertion of a retroelement, potentially through mechanisms like ectopic recombination.

name: Post-transcriptional defense
description: Post-transcriptional defense mechanisms suppress transposable element activity after transcription, such as through RNA interference.

name: Post-transcriptional gene regulation
description: Post-transcriptional gene regulation refers to the control of gene expression after the RNA transcript has been made.

name: Post-transcriptional regulation
description: Post-transcriptional regulation involves processes that control RNA metabolism after transcription, such as modification, stability, and translation.

name: Post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression
description: Post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression involves mechanisms that control gene expression after transcription has occurred.

name: Post-translational regulation
description: Post-translational regulation refers to the control of protein activity or stability after translation.

name: Post-translational regulation of L1 activity
description: Post-translational regulation of L1 activity refers to the potential control of LINE-1 function through modifications after protein synthesis.

name: Post-translational regulation of retrotransposition
description: Post-translational regulation of retrotransposition refers to cellular mechanisms that control L1 activity after the L1 proteins have been synthesized.

name: Precise deletion
description: Precise deletion refers to the exact removal of a previously established retrotransposon insertion.

name: Precise parallel insertion
description: Precise parallel insertion refers to the independent insertion of apparently identical retrotransposons at orthologous positions in different lineages.

name: Precise transposon excision
description: Precise transposon excision is the clean removal of a transposon from its original genomic location during transposition.

name: Predisposing to genomic instability
description: The action of increasing the susceptibility of a genomic region to structural changes or mutations.

name: Preimplantation embryo development
description: Preimplantation embryo development refers to the early stages of embryonic development before implantation in the uterus.

name: Premature insertion mechanism
description: Premature insertion mechanism refers to the process where a transposon insertion event is terminated before completion, often resulting in truncated sequences.

name: Premature stop codon introduction
description: Premature stop codon introduction is a molecular consequence where a mutation or aberrant splicing creates a stop codon earlier than the normal termination site, leading to a truncated protein.

name: Premature termination codon introduction
description: Premature termination codon introduction is the creation of a stop codon within the coding sequence, leading to truncated protein products.

name: Preservation of naive pluripotency
description: The maintenance of the earliest, most primitive state of pluripotency in stem cells.

name: Prevention of developmental reversion
description: Prevention of developmental reversion refers to the mechanism that blocks the reversion of naive human embryonic stem cells to an earlier developmental state, such as 8-cell-like cells.

name: Primate identification
description: The process of determining the primate species from which a DNA sample or tissue originated.

name: Pro-inflammatory cytokine production
description: Pro-inflammatory cytokine production is the synthesis of signaling molecules that promote inflammation.

name: Pro-oncogenic perturbation
description: Pro-oncogenic perturbation refers to genomic alterations that promote the initiation or progression of cancer.

name: Processed pseudogene generation
description: Processed pseudogene generation is a mechanism where host messenger RNA is reverse-transcribed and integrated into the genome, creating a non-functional copy of a gene.

name: Processed pseudogene genesis
description: The process by which processed pseudogenes are generated through retrotransposition of mRNA transcripts.

name: Processive DNA synthesis
description: Processive DNA synthesis describes the ability of a polymerase to incorporate many nucleotides without dissociating from the template; L1 RT is shown to be highly processive.

name: Promoter DNA methylation
description: Promoter DNA methylation is an epigenetic modification that typically represses gene or transposon expression.

name: Promoter acquisition
description: Promoter acquisition is the process by which a gene or retroelement gains regulatory sequences to enable its transcription.

name: Promoter activity in vivo
description: L1 ASP can act as a promoter in living organisms.

name: Promoter activity modulation by flanking sequence
description: The process by which upstream flanking cellular sequences strongly influence the L1 5'-UTR promoter activity, either repressing or enhancing it.

name: Promoter activity of LINE-1 5' UTR
description: The promoter activity of the LINE-1 5' untranslated region (UTR) drives the transcription of LINE-1 RNA, which is the first step in the retrotransposition cycle.

name: Promoter activity regulation
description: The process of controlling the level of transcription initiation from a gene promoter.

name: Promoter activity regulation of LINE-1 5'-UTR
description: Promoter activity regulation of LINE-1 5'-UTR refers to the control of transcription initiation from the internal promoter located within the 5' untranslated region of LINE-1.

name: Promoter and first exon function
description: The process by which a transposon element functions as the promoter and first exon of a protein-coding gene.

name: Promoter binding
description: The process where a transcription factor binds to gene promoter regions to regulate transcription.

name: Promoter blocking
description: Promoter blocking refers to the inhibition of transcriptional activity from a gene or element's promoter region.

name: Promoter capture
description: Promoter capture is a mechanism by which transposable elements acquire and utilize host gene promoters to drive their own expression.

name: Promoter creation
description: Promoter creation refers to the formation of novel transcriptional start sites.

name: Promoter divergence
description: The process by which the regulatory regions of duplicate genes become functionally distinct.

name: Promoter function
description: Promoter function refers to the ability of a DNA sequence to initiate transcription of a gene.

name: Promoter methylation
description: Promoter methylation is an epigenetic modification that typically leads to gene silencing by inhibiting transcription factor binding.

name: Promoter recruitment
description: The process by which a transposable element is independently co-opted to serve as an alternative promoter for gene expression.

name: Promoting cancer cell invasion and metastasis
description: The process by which cancer cells acquire the ability to invade surrounding tissues and spread to distant sites.

name: Promotion of LINE-1 retrotransposition
description: The process of increasing the rate or activity of LINE-1 retrotransposition.

name: Protein evolution
description: Protein evolution refers to the process by which changes in protein structure or function arise, often through genetic alterations.

name: Protein multimerization
description: Protein multimerization is the process by which multiple protein subunits assemble into a larger complex.

name: Protein truncation
description: Protein truncation is the production of a shortened, often non-functional, protein product.

name: Protein-coding gene evolution
description: The process through which LTRs contribute to the creation of new protein-coding genes by providing regulatory and coding sequences.

name: Protein-coding region formation
description: The process by which repeat sequences become functional elements, such as protein-coding regions.

name: Protein-protein interaction
description: Protein-protein interaction is the physical association between two or more protein molecules.

name: Protein-protein interaction screening
description: A method to systematically identify proteins that physically interact with a protein of interest.

name: Providing a fragile site for double-strand breaks
description: Providing a fragile site for double-strand breaks refers to creating genomic locations that are prone to DNA double-strand breaks.

name: Providing accessible chromatin regions
description: The function of contributing to genomic regions that are open and accessible for transcription factor binding and gene regulation.

name: Providing breakpoints
description: Providing breakpoints refers to the role of a sequence in facilitating genomic rearrangements by serving as a site for DNA breakage and rejoining.

name: Providing complete exon cassettes
description: The contribution of sequences that constitute entire, functional exons.

name: Providing novel promoters
description: The process by which transposable elements supply new promoter regions to host genes upon integration.

name: Providing novel splicing sites
description: The process by which transposable elements introduce new splice donor or acceptor sites into host genes upon integration.

name: Providing promoters
description: The contribution of regulatory sequences that initiate transcription of a gene.

name: Providing splice donors and acceptors
description: The contribution of sequences that define the boundaries of introns for splicing.

name: Providing stop codon and polyadenylation signal
description: A process where a genetic element contributes sequences that signal the termination of translation (stop codon) and the addition of a poly(A) tail to mRNA (polyadenylation signal).

name: Providing terminal exons
description: The contribution of sequences that form the final exon of a transcript, often containing the stop codon and polyadenylation signal.

name: Providing transcription termination sites
description: The act of supplying genomic sequences that serve as signals for the termination of transcription.

name: Proviral integration
description: Proviral integration is the process by which a retroviral DNA copy is inserted into the host genome.

name: Provision of STOP codons
description: Provision of STOP codons refers to the introduction of nucleotide triplets that signal the termination of protein synthesis.

name: Provision of mRNA splicing signals
description: Provision of mRNA splicing signals refers to the supply of sequences that guide the removal of introns and joining of exons in pre-mRNA.

name: Provision of promoters and regulatory sequences
description: Provision of promoters and regulatory sequences refers to the contribution of genetic elements that control the initiation and regulation of gene transcription.

name: Provision of transcription initiation signals
description: Provision of transcription initiation signals refers to the supply of sequences that mark the start site for RNA synthesis.

name: Pseudoexon insertion
description: Pseudoexon insertion is the incorporation of a cryptic or non-canonical exon into a mature mRNA transcript.

name: Pseudogene creation
description: The generation of non-functional copies of genes through retrotransposition.

name: Pseudogene formation
description: Pseudogene formation is a process where non-functional copies of genes are generated, in this case mediated by L1 reverse transcriptase activity.

name: Pseudogenization
description: Pseudogenization is the process by which a functional gene loses its protein-coding ability, often due to mutations.

name: Purifying selection
description: Purifying selection is a type of natural selection that acts to remove deleterious genetic variants from a population.

name: Purkinje cell degeneration
description: Purkinje cell degeneration is the loss of Purkinje neurons in the cerebellum, a key pathological feature in several ataxias.

name: Quantification
description: Quantification refers to the measurement of the amount or concentration of a specific target, such as human-derived cfDNA, in a biological sample.

name: R-loop formation
description: The formation of RNA-DNA hybrids, which are key intermediates during LINE-1 retrotransposition, including cis L1 R-loops (L1 mRNA-genomic DNA) and trans L1 R-loops (L1 mRNA-complementary DNA).

name: R-loop形成
description: 由RNA-DNA杂交体（如L1 mRNA与基因组DNA或互补DNA形成的杂交体）和一条被置换的DNA单链组成的三链结构。

name: RBP association
description: The binding of RNA Binding Proteins to specific sequences within RNA transcripts, influencing their fate and function.

name: RIG-I-like receptor (RLR)-associated type-I interferon signaling
description: RIG-I-like receptor (RLR)-associated type-I interferon signaling is an innate immune pathway triggered by detection of viral or endogenous dsRNA.

name: RIG-I-mediated RNA sensing
description: 一种RNA感应通路，在本研究中被证明参与介导LINE-1触发的免疫激活。

name: RNA 3'-end polyadenylation
description: RNA 3'-end polyadenylation is the addition of a poly(A) tail to the 3' end of an RNA molecule.

name: RNA 3'-end uridylation
description: RNA 3'-end uridylation is the addition of uridine residues to the 3' end of an RNA molecule.

name: RNA 3'末端多聚腺苷酸化
description: RNA 3'末端多聚腺苷酸化是RNA成熟和稳定性的关键步骤，也参与逆转录转座子RNA的调控。

name: RNA 3'末端尿苷化
description: RNA 3'末端尿苷化是一种RNA修饰，通常标记RNA以进行降解，参与逆转录转座子RNA的调控。

name: RNA binding
description: LINE-1 ORF1 protein (ORF1p) is an RNA-binding protein that binds to L1 RNA.

name: RNA chaperone activity
description: LINE-1 ORF1 protein (ORF1p) acts as an RNA chaperone, which is required for retrotransposition.

name: RNA circularization
description: RNA circularization is a process where the ends of an RNA molecule are joined to form a circle.

name: RNA circularization via Alu/Alu base-pairing
description: A process where Alu sequences located in flanking introns base-pair with each other, facilitating the formation of circular RNA molecules from exons.

name: RNA decay
description: RNA decay is the process of RNA degradation.

name: RNA degradation
description: RNA degradation is a cellular pathway that breaks down RNA molecules, serving as a regulatory mechanism to control the expression of retrotransposons.

name: RNA duplex formation
description: RNA duplex formation is the process where complementary RNA strands base-pair to form a double-stranded structure.

name: RNA expression
description: The process of transcribing DNA sequences into RNA molecules.

name: RNA interference (RNAi)
description: RNA interference is a mechanism of gene silencing triggered by small interfering RNAs.

name: RNA interference (RNAi) by antisense ORF0 RNA
description: RNA interference by antisense ORF0 RNA is a potential regulatory mechanism targeting LINE-1 RNA.

name: RNA ligation
description: A known mechanism potentially involved in the formation of LINE-1 chimeric insertions.

name: RNA m6A modification
description: A common post-transcriptional RNA modification, involving methylation at the N6 position of adenosine.

name: RNA m6A修饰
description: RNA m6A修饰是一种常见的RNA表观遗传修饰，涉及甲基化酶（writer）和去甲基化酶（eraser）的调控。

name: RNA metabolism
description: A recurring theme for retrotransposons is their tie-in to RNA metabolism, as they mobilize via RNA intermediates.

name: RNA methylation
description: RNA methylation is a chemical modification of RNA molecules, such as N6-methyladenosine (m6A).

name: RNA packaging
description: RNA packaging is a function performed by the L1ORF1 protein during LINE-1 retrotransposition.

name: RNA polymerase II activity
description: The transcriptional activity of RNA polymerase II, the enzyme responsible for synthesizing mRNA and other non-coding RNAs.

name: RNA polymerase II enrichment
description: The accumulation of RNA polymerase II at specific genomic loci.

name: RNA polymerase II transcription
description: A transcription process regulated by motifs such as CCAAT, TATA, and ATTAAA found in long terminal repeats.

name: RNA polymerase II-driven transcription
description: Transcription of LINE-1 elements is driven by an internal RNA polymerase II promoter.

name: RNA polymerase III transcription
description: RNA polymerase III transcription is the process by which RNA polymerase III synthesizes small, non-coding RNAs, including some from transposable elements.

name: RNA polymerase III-dependent transcription
description: A transcription process mediated by RNA polymerase III, which is sensitive to the inhibitor tagetitoxin.

name: RNA polymerase III-driven transcription
description: Transcription of SINE elements like Alu is driven by an internal RNA polymerase III promoter.

name: RNA processing
description: RNA processing encompasses the modifications an RNA molecule undergoes after transcription, such as splicing, editing, and polyadenylation.

name: RNA quality control
description: RNA quality control refers to cellular mechanisms that ensure the fidelity and proper processing of RNA molecules.

name: RNA recognition by LINE-1 reverse transcriptase
description: The mechanism by which the LINE-1-encoded reverse transcriptase (RT) specifically recognizes its own RNA and Alu RNA for retrotransposition.

name: RNA recombination
description: RNA recombination is a process involving the exchange of genetic material between RNA molecules.

name: RNA recruitment to L1-RNP
description: The RNA-binding property of ORF1p is critical to recruit other mobile RNAs to the L1-ribonucleoprotein particle.

name: RNA secondary structure disruption
description: RNA secondary structure disruption refers to the alteration of the base-pairing patterns within an RNA molecule, which can affect its function and interactions.

name: RNA sensing pathways
description: RNA sensing pathways are innate immune signaling cascades that detect foreign or aberrant RNA, such as those mediated by RIG-I and MDA5.

name: RNA splicing
description: RNA splicing is the process of removing introns from a pre-mRNA transcript and joining exons together.

name: RNA stability
description: The resistance of an RNA molecule to degradation, influencing its availability for processes like retrotransposition.

name: RNA stability regulation
description: RNA stability regulation refers to the processes that control the degradation or stabilization of RNA molecules, influencing their half-life and abundance.

name: RNA surveillance
description: RNA surveillance refers to cellular mechanisms that monitor and degrade aberrant or nonsense RNAs.

name: RNA targeting
description: RNA targeting refers to the process of directing or localizing RNA molecules to specific cellular locations or complexes.

name: RNA template switching
description: A process during reverse transcription where the reverse transcriptase switches from one RNA template to another, leading to the formation of chimeric cDNA.

name: RNA transcript production
description: RNA transcript production is the generation of RNA molecules from a DNA sequence, which is a key step in gene expression.

name: RNA truncation
description: RNA truncation refers to the generation of shortened RNA transcripts, often due to premature termination of transcription.

name: RNA-DNA hybrid formation
description: RNA-DNA hybrid formation is the generation of structures where an RNA strand is base-paired with a complementary DNA strand.

name: RNA-DNA杂交体形成
description: 在逆转录转座过程中，LINE-1 RNA与基因组DNA或新合成的cDNA之间形成的异源双链结构。

name: RNA-RNA interaction
description: RNA-RNA interaction refers to the direct physical association between different RNA molecules.

name: RNA-RNA recombination
description: RNA-RNA recombination is a process that occurs during L1 reverse transcription, leading to the formation of chimeric retrotranscripts.

name: RNA-chromatin crosstalk
description: RNA-chromatin crosstalk describes the interaction and mutual regulation between RNA molecules and chromatin structure during cell fate transitions.

name: RNA-guided transposition
description: RNA-guided transposition is a process where transposase activity is directed to specific genomic sequences using guide RNAs.

name: RNA-mediated DNA repair
description: A proposed ancestral mechanism associated with non-LTR retrotransposons that may have been used before the acquisition of an endonuclease domain.

name: RNA-mediated gene insertion
description: RNA-mediated gene insertion is a process where genetic material is delivered and integrated into the genome via an RNA intermediate.

name: RNA-mediated genomic invasion
description: The process by which retrotransposon RNAs and cellular mRNAs are reverse transcribed and integrated into genomic DNA.

name: RNA-protein complex formation
description: The process where SINE-derived RNAs interact with cellular proteins to form ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes.

name: RNA-sensing response
description: An innate immune response triggered by sensing of RNA, mediated through MAVS signaling.

name: RNA-seq analysis
description: A method using next-generation sequencing to measure RNA expression levels.

name: RNA/DNA hybrid processing
description: RNA/DNA hybrid processing refers to the cleavage of the RNA strand in RNA/DNA hybrids, a function shared by RNase H1 and RNase H2.

name: RNA:DNA heteroduplex formation
description: RNA:DNA heteroduplex formation is the process where an RNA strand base-pairs with a complementary DNA strand.

name: RNA:DNA hybrid formation
description: RNA:DNA hybrid formation is the generation of duplex structures composed of one RNA strand and one DNA strand.

name: RNA:DNA hybrid production
description: RNA:DNA杂交体产生是ORF2p在细胞质中逆转录活动产生的副产物。

name: RNA二级结构识别
description: LINE-1编码的逆转录酶通过识别RNA分子（如自身或Alu RNA）的特定二级结构（如茎环结构）来启动逆转录转座。

name: RNA伴侣活性
description: L1-ORF1p蛋白帮助LINE-1 RNA正确折叠和维持功能构象的能力。

name: RNA剪接
description: LINE-1元件包含多个功能性剪接供体和受体位点，导致其mRNA发生广泛而复杂的剪接。

name: RNA加工
description: 对LINE-1转录本进行剪接等处理的过程，是L1活性的主要调节方式。

name: RNA包装
description: L1ORF1蛋白通过其结构域特异性识别并结合单链RNA底物，形成核糖核蛋白复合物，为逆转录转座做准备。

name: RNA和蛋白积累减少
description: 锌指抗病毒蛋白（ZAP）通过降低全长LINE-1 RNA及其编码蛋白的积累来抑制逆转录转座。

name: RNA外切体复合物介导的RNA降解
description: RNA外切体复合物是一种多亚基核糖核酸酶复合物，能够降解多种RNA分子，并与转座子控制相关。

name: RNA干扰
description: RNA干扰是一种通过小RNA分子抑制基因表达的机制。

name: RNA提前多聚腺苷酸化
description: LINE-1的A富集编码链包含多个功能性的典型和非典型多聚腺苷酸化信号，导致全长转录本被截断。

name: RNA来源的附加体产物
description: 由LINE-1逆转录转座产生的、未整合到染色体中的环状DNA分子。

name: RNA甲基化
description: 发生在RNA分子上的甲基化修饰，如m6A修饰。

name: RNA结合
description: LINE-1的ORF1蛋白具有结合RNA的能力，这是其形成核糖核蛋白颗粒和进行逆转录转座所必需的活性。

name: RNA聚合酶II驱动转录
description: RNA聚合酶II驱动转录是指由RNA聚合酶II启动子（如CMV）启动的转录过程，影响逆转录元件的转座动力学。

name: RNA脱帽
description: 由DCP2酶介导的去除mRNA 5'端帽子结构的过程，通常导致RNA降解。

name: RNA表达增加
description: 特定基因或序列的转录水平升高。

name: RNA质量控制
description: 确保RNA分子正确性和功能的过程，是L1-SIRT6相互作用网络富集的通路之一。

name: RNA连接
description: RNA连接酶RtcB在体外将具有2‘，3’-环磷酸末端的U6 RNA与具有5‘-OH的L1 RNA连接起来。

name: RNA遗传
description: 在生殖细胞中转录的RNA可以通过受精过程传递到胚胎，并在胚胎期介导整合，这是一种独立于其编码DNA的遗传方式。

name: RNA降解
description: RNA降解是细胞清除不需要的RNA（包括逆转录转座子RNA）的重要机制。

name: RNA靶向干扰
description: APOBEC3蛋白可能通过干扰LINE-1 RNA的靶向或功能来间接抑制其转座。

name: RNP complex assembly
description: RNP complex assembly refers to the formation of ribonucleoprotein particles containing L1 RNA and the encoded proteins ORF1p and ORF2p.

name: RNP complex formation
description: ORF1p packages its transcript into an ORF2p-containing ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex, which is the retrotransposition intermediate.

name: RNP formation
description: RNP (ribonucleoprotein) formation is the assembly of LINE-1 RNA with its encoded proteins, which is a critical step in the retrotransposition cycle.

name: RNP sequestration in cytoplasmic bodies
description: RNP sequestration in cytoplasmic bodies involves the compartmentalization of ribonucleoprotein complexes, potentially to limit their activity.

name: RNP凝聚物形成
description: 多个ORF1p拷贝组装到LINE-1 RNA上形成核糖核蛋白凝聚物。

name: RNP复合物组装
description: LINE-1 RNA与ORF1p和ORF2p蛋白相互作用，形成功能性的核糖核蛋白复合物，这是逆转录转座的关键步骤。

name: RNP形成
description: LINE-1 RNA与ORF1p和ORF2p结合形成核糖核蛋白复合体的过程，这对逆转录转座至关重要。

name: RNP运输
description: LINE-1核糖核蛋白复合体在细胞内向细胞核移动的过程。

name: RNP隔离
description: 逆转录转座子核糖核蛋白复合物被隔离在细胞质凝聚体中，以限制其活性。

name: RNase H activity
description: A putative function associated with the 3'-end domain of the LINE ORF2 protein, dispensable for reverse transcription in this assay.

name: RNase H2降解LINE-1 RNA
description: 逆转录后，RNase H2降解LINE-1 RNA，从而促进逆转录转座完成。

name: RT burden
description: 样本中逆转录转座事件的数量或负荷。

name: RT inhibition
description: 逆转录酶抑制，通过抑制逆转录酶活性来影响细胞调控，是逆转录酶抑制剂发挥细胞毒性的核心机制。

name: RT-dependent mechanism in tumorigenesis
description: A mechanism that operates in tumorigenesis and is dependent on reverse transcriptase activity.

name: RT-dependent regulatory mechanism
description: A regulatory mechanism that operates during preimplantation development, is repressed in normal tissues, and when reactivated, promotes cell transformation and cancer progression.

name: RT-mediated somatic gene recombination/retroinsertion
description: RT-mediated somatic gene recombination/retroinsertion is a process where reverse transcriptase activity facilitates the insertion or recombination of genetic material in somatic cells.

name: RTE copy number accumulation
description: 逆转录转座元件拷贝数随时间的增加。

name: RTE de-repression
description: RTE de-repression is the loss of normal silencing mechanisms, leading to increased activity of retrotransposable elements.

name: RUNX3与L1Hs结合
description: 指转录因子RUNX3特异性结合到L1Hs 5'UTR的RUNX位点。

name: Random insertion
description: Random insertion refers to the distribution pattern of recent L1 insertions in genomic DNA.

name: Randomized mutagenesis
description: Randomized mutagenesis is a technique for introducing random mutations at a specific genomic locus to study protein function, regulatory elements, or model genetic variations.

name: Rapid evolution
description: A high rate of sequence change over evolutionary time, characteristic of certain genomic elements.

name: Reactivation of embryonic transcription profiles
description: Reactivation of embryonic transcription profiles refers to the re-expression of gene expression patterns characteristic of embryonic development.

name: Rearrangement
description: Rearrangement refers to structural changes in the genome, such as deletions, duplications, inversions, or translocations.

name: Recent transposition
description: Recent transposition refers to the movement of a transposable element within the genome that has occurred in the recent evolutionary past.

name: Receptor for insertion
description: A receptor for insertion is a genomic site or sequence that serves as a target for the integration of other transposons.

name: Reciprocal regulation
description: Reciprocal regulation describes a mutual regulatory relationship between two or more molecular entities, such as between LINE-1 and transcription factors.

name: Reciprocal regulatory influence
description: The reciprocal regulatory influence refers to the bidirectional interaction between individual L1 copies and their genomic integration sites.

name: Recombination cold spot
description: A recombination cold spot is a genomic region where the frequency of meiotic recombination is significantly lower than the genome average.

name: Recombination-mediated integration
description: Recombination-mediated integration is a mechanism for the insertion of genetic material, such as a circular intermediate, into the genome.

name: Recombinational editing
description: A proposed mechanism for the nonrandom genomic distribution of transposons involving recombination events.

name: Recruitment of transcription factors
description: The process by which retrotransposons bind and attract specific transcription factors.

name: Recruitment of transcriptional repressors
description: The process by which retrotransposons bind and attract proteins that suppress gene transcription.

name: Reduced primary transcript content
description: Decreased content of the corresponding hnRNAs (heterogeneous nuclear RNAs) due to specific transposon insertions.

name: Regenerative therapy
description: Regenerative therapy involves the use of cells, genes, or biomaterials to repair, replace, or regenerate damaged tissues or organs.

name: Regional mutation pressure
description: Regional mutation pressure refers to the tendency for repetitive sequences to be subject to the same mutation pressure as their surrounding genomic regions, leading to similar GC content.

name: Regional silencing
description: Regional silencing is a mechanism where gene or transposon repression occurs preferentially over a defined chromosomal region rather than genome-wide.

name: Regulating cell interactions during inflammatory reaction
description: A biological process involving the control of communication and responses between cells in the context of inflammation.

name: Regulating gene expression
description: Regulating gene expression is the process of controlling the amount and timing of gene product production.

name: Regulation by DNA methylation
description: Regulation by DNA methylation refers to the control of gene or repeat expression through the addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases in DNA.

name: Regulation during early embryonic development
description: Regulation during early embryonic development refers to the control of retrotransposon expression and activity in the context of early embryogenesis.

name: Regulation in inflammatory settings
description: The control of LTR retrotransposon expression levels in the context of inflammation.

name: Regulation of TE activity
description: Mechanisms that control the expression and mobilization of transposable elements.

name: Regulation of expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL)
description: The process by which genetic variants influence the expression levels of genes or other transcripts.

name: Regulation of gene expression in immune response
description: The process by which gene expression is controlled during the immune response to infection.

name: Regulation of human genes
description: Regulation of human genes refers to the control of gene expression, which can be influenced by nearby genomic elements like ERVs/LRs.

name: Regulation of pluripotency states
description: Regulation of pluripotency states refers to the molecular control of distinct pluripotent cell states.

name: Regulation of protein-coding gene expression
description: The process by which SINE retrotransposons impact the expression of protein-coding genes.

name: Regulation of retrotransposable elements
description: Regulation of retrotransposable elements refers to the cellular mechanisms that control the expression and activity of retrotransposons.

name: Regulation of retrotransposons
description: Regulation of retrotransposons involves cellular silencing mechanisms that restrict the expression and mobility of retrotransposable elements to prevent genomic damage.

name: Regulation of stochastic fluctuations
description: Regulation of stochastic fluctuations refers to the control of random variations in the copy numbers of transposable elements.

name: Regulation of target genes
description: The process by which miRNAs affect the expression of various target genes in the genome.

name: Regulation of transcription
description: The control of the rate at which genetic information is copied from DNA to RNA.

name: Regulation of transcription initiation
description: The process by which the start of gene transcription is controlled, which can be influenced by genomic repeat elements.

name: Regulation of transposable element numbers
description: Regulation of transposable element numbers is a proposed mechanism where nonautonomous elements control the copy numbers of autonomous elements.

name: Regulatory element
description: A regulatory element is a DNA sequence that controls the transcription of one or more genes.

name: Regulatory element acquisition
description: The process where LINE-1 loci with defunct promoters can acquire regulatory elements from nearby genes to restore retrotransposition activity.

name: Regulatory evolution
description: The evolutionary changes in the genomic regulatory landscape, including promoters and enhancers, across species or tissues.

name: Regulatory landscape remodeling
description: The process by which the three-dimensional organization and regulatory interactions of a genomic region are altered.

name: Regulatory network assembly
description: Regulatory network assembly refers to the process of integrating various DNA sequences, including transposon-derived sequences, into cellular gene regulatory circuits.

name: Regulatory protein encoding
description: Regulatory protein encoding refers to the process where a transposon encodes a protein that can regulate biological processes.

name: Regulatory role
description: A regulatory role refers to the function of a genomic element in controlling gene expression or other cellular processes.

name: Regulatory roles
description: Regulatory roles refer to the functions of transposable elements in controlling gene expression or other genomic processes.

name: Regulatory signature switching
description: The evolutionary process where a genomic region changes its primary regulatory function, such as switching between promoter and enhancer activity.

name: Reinfection
description: Reinfection refers to the process by which exogenous retroviruses can integrate into the germline, contributing to the formation of HERVs.

name: Relative expression
description: Relative expression refers to the quantification of gene or element expression levels compared to a reference or control group.

name: Repair of double-strand breaks
description: Repair of double-strand breaks (DSBs) is a cellular process to fix DNA breaks, which can be mediated by reverse transcriptase.

name: Repeat element dysregulation
description: Repeat element dysregulation refers to the abnormal expression or suppression of repetitive sequences, including transposons, in pathological states.

name: Repeat element expression
description: Repeat element expression refers to the transcription of repetitive sequences, including transposons, in the genome.

name: Repeat enrichment in gene promoters
description: Repeat enrichment in gene promoters refers to the overrepresentation of repetitive elements in the regulatory regions of genes.

name: Repeat expansion
description: The pathological increase in the number of tandem repeat sequences, often associated with neurological diseases.

name: Repeat expression
description: Repeat expression refers to the transcriptional activity of repetitive DNA elements, including transposons.

name: Repeat-induced gene activation (RIGA)
description: Repeat-induced gene activation is a process where repeated sequences enhance gene expression.

name: Repeat-induced gene silencing (RIGS)
description: Repeat-induced gene silencing is a process where repeated sequences suppress gene expression.

name: Repetitive element transcript expression
description: The transcription of repetitive element sequences into RNA.

name: Replication mechanism
description: The process by which retroposons copy and insert themselves into new genomic locations.

name: Replication origin association
description: Replication origin association refers to the localization of genomic elements within or near constitutive replication origins.

name: Replication regulation
description: Replication regulation refers to the control of the replication process of genetic elements, such as retroelements.

name: Replicative senescence
description: A state of irreversible cell cycle arrest associated with aging and cellular stress.

name: Repression
description: Repression is a form of gene or transposon expression regulation that decreases or silences transcription.

name: Repression by DNA methylation
description: The silencing of gene or transposon expression through the addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases in DNA.

name: Repression by KRAB-ZNF proteins
description: Repression by KRAB-ZNF proteins is a key defense mechanism in mammals that silences transposable elements to offer genomic protection.

name: Repression of L1 5' UTR
description: HBV polymerase represses the activity of the L1 5' untranslated region.

name: Repression of proximal genes
description: The process of decreasing the transcription of genes located nearby.

name: Repression of retrotransposons
description: Repression of retrotransposons is the silencing mechanism that controls their activity.

name: Repression of transposable elements
description: Repression of transposable elements refers to the epigenetic silencing of transposons to prevent their aberrant activity.

name: Reprogramming
description: Reprogramming refers to the process of converting somatic cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) by expressing specific factors.

name: Reprogramming of gene expression
description: Reprogramming of gene expression refers to the alteration of gene expression patterns.

name: Reprogramming transgene removal
description: The process of excising transposon-delivered reprogramming transgenes from the genome of induced pluripotent stem cells.

name: Reprogramming vector removal
description: Reprogramming vector removal is a technique to eliminate exogenous genetic material used to induce pluripotency, ensuring the safety of the resulting iPSCs.

name: Reshaping the genome
description: The process by which retrotransposons alter the structure and composition of the genome.

name: Restriction
description: Restriction refers to the inhibition or limitation of the mobility or infectivity of retroelements and retroviruses.

name: Retroduplication
description: Retroduplication is the process involving reverse transcription of mRNAs and their insertion back into the genome.

name: Retroelement silencing
description: Retroelement silencing refers to the epigenetic and transcriptional mechanisms that repress the activity of retrotransposable elements in the genome.

name: Retrogene formation
description: Retrogene formation is the creation of new genes through the retroposition of processed mRNA.

name: Retroposition
description: Retroposition is the process by which retrotransposons insert new copies into the genome via an RNA intermediate.

name: Retrotransposable element (RTE) control
description: Retrotransposable element control refers to the cellular mechanisms, such as siRNA silencing, that repress the activity of retrotransposons in somatic tissues.

name: Retrotransposition control
description: Retrotransposition control refers to the host mechanisms that regulate or suppress the mobility of retrotransposable elements.

name: Retrotransposition in cis
description: Retrotransposition in cis is the process by which LINE-1 preferentially mobilizes its own encoding RNA.

name: Retrotransposition in trans
description: Retrotransposition in trans is the process by which LINE-1 proteins can mobilize non-self RNAs, such as Alu RNA or cellular mRNAs.

name: Retrotransposition of endogenous genes
description: Retrotransposition of endogenous genes refers to the process where cellular protein-coding genes are reverse-transcribed and reinserted into the genome, creating processed pseudogenes.

name: Retrotransposition of processed pseudogenes
description: The process by which cellular mRNAs are reverse transcribed and inserted into the nuclear genome by L1 machinery, creating processed pseudogenes.

name: Retrotransposon Insertion Polymorphism (RIP)
description: Retrotransposon Insertion Polymorphism (RIP) is defined as the presence or absence of a retrotransposon insertion among human populations at a specific genomic location.

name: Retrotransposon RNA stability regulation
description: Retrotransposon RNA stability regulation is the process by which the stability of retrotransposon RNA transcripts is controlled, affecting their potential for retrotransposition.

name: Retrotransposon abundance variation
description: Retrotransposon abundance variation refers to differences in the copy number or genomic proportion of retrotransposons among individuals or populations.

name: Retrotransposon activity
description: Retrotransposon activity refers to the transcription and mobilization of retrotransposons within the genome.

name: Retrotransposon amplification
description: Retrotransposon amplification refers to the process of increasing copy number through replication and insertion.

name: Retrotransposon control
description: Retrotransposon control refers to the cellular mechanisms that regulate the activity and mobility of retrotransposons.

name: Retrotransposon diversification
description: The evolutionary process leading to the emergence of distinct retrotransposon lineages or families.

name: Retrotransposon evolution
description: Retrotransposon evolution refers to the process of change and diversification of retrotransposon families and subfamilies over time.

name: Retrotransposon expression upregulation
description: An increase in the expression levels of retrotransposons.

name: Retrotransposon extinction
description: The loss of active retrotransposon lineages from a genome.

name: Retrotransposon insertion
description: Retrotransposon insertion refers to the genomic integration event of a retrotransposon into a new location in the genome.

name: Retrotransposon insertion polymorphism
description: Retrotransposon insertion polymorphism refers to the presence or absence of non-reference transposable element variants in an individual's genome.

name: Retrotransposon mobilization
description: Retrotransposon mobilization is the process by which retrotransposons move to new genomic locations.

name: Retrotransposon proliferation
description: The process by which retrotransposons increase their copy number within the host genome, creating a tension with host survival.

name: Retrotransposon propagation
description: Retrotransposon propagation refers to the process by which retrotransposons, such as LINE-1, replicate and increase their copy number within the genome through a copy-and-paste mechanism.

name: Retrotransposon recombination-mediated inversion (RRMI)
description: Retrotransposon recombination-mediated inversion is a mechanism that causes chromosomal inversions without altering the amount of genomic material.

name: Retrotransposon silencing
description: Retrotransposon silencing refers to the cellular mechanisms that restrict the mobility and expression of retrotransposons.

name: Retrotransposon transcription
description: Retrotransposon transcription is the process of generating RNA transcripts from retrotransposon sequences.

name: Retrotransposon transcription regulation
description: Retrotransposon transcription regulation involves the control of the initiation and rate of transcription of retrotransposon elements from genomic DNA into RNA.

name: Retrotransposon transcriptional silencing
description: A host defense mechanism to repress retrotransposon transcription and prevent further insertions.

name: Retrotransposon-mediated sequence transduction
description: Retrotransposon-mediated sequence transduction is the process by which a retrotransposon carries flanking sequence during its mobilization, proposed as a gene duplication mechanism.

name: Retroviral deletion
description: Retroviral deletion refers to the loss of endogenous retroviral sequences from the genome, which can occur over evolutionary time.

name: Retroviral insertion
description: Retroviral insertion is the process by which a retrovirus integrates its genetic material into the host genome.

name: Retroviral insertion mutagenesis
description: A process where the integration of a retroviral genome into the host DNA disrupts gene function or regulation, leading to mutations.

name: Retroviral integration
description: Retroviral integration is the process by which a retrovirus inserts its DNA copy into the host genome.

name: Retroviral primer-binding site hybridization
description: A method using synthetic oligonucleotides complementary to putative retroviral primer-binding sites as hybridization probes to detect novel retrovirus-like sequences.

name: Retroviral provirus structure
description: A genomic element with structural features characteristic of a retroviral provirus, including long terminal repeats and a pol gene.

name: Retroviral transposition
description: Retroviral transposition is a process by which retroviral-like elements move and insert into new genomic locations.

name: Reverse transcriptase activity
description: Reverse transcriptase activity is the enzymatic function of converting RNA into DNA, performed by the protein encoded by L1 ORF2.

name: Reverse transcriptase expression
description: Reverse transcriptase expression refers to the production of the enzyme that catalyzes the reverse transcription of RNA into DNA.

name: Reverse transcriptase-mediated DNA damage response
description: Reverse transcriptase-mediated DNA damage response is a cellular response triggered by the reverse transcriptase activity of LINE-1 ORF2p, independent of its endonuclease activity.

name: Reverse transcription and integration
description: Reverse transcription and integration is the process by which RNA is reverse transcribed into DNA and then inserted into a new genomic location.

name: Reverse transcription by ORF2p
description: ORF2p possesses reverse transcriptase activity and can use recruited cellular RNAs as templates.

name: Reverse transcription of L1 RNA 5'-CAP
description: A process that explains the higher frequency of G nucleotides observed upstream from the L1, likely involving the incorporation of the RNA cap during reverse transcription.

name: Ribonuclease sensitivity
description: The property of a complex being disrupted or dissociated upon treatment with ribonuclease.

name: Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex formation
description: The process where L1-encoded proteins ORF1p and ORF2p bind to L1 RNA in cis to form a ribonucleoprotein complex essential for retrotransposition.

name: Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) condensate formation
description: Many copies of the ORF1p protein assemble onto the LINE-1 RNA to form a ribonucleoprotein (RNP) condensate.

name: Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) formation
description: Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) formation is the process where the full-length LINE-1 transcript assembles in cis with its encoded proteins ORF1p and ORF2p.

name: Ribonucleoprotein (RNP) particle formation
description: The process of forming complexes containing retrotransposon RNA and proteins, such as ORF1p and ORF2p, which are essential for retrotransposition.

name: Ribonucleoprotein complex formation
description: Ribonucleoprotein complex formation is the process by which RNA and proteins assemble into a functional complex.

name: Ribonucleoprotein particle (RNP) formation
description: The process where L1-encoded proteins (ORF1p and ORF2p) preferentially associate with their encoding transcript to form a proposed retrotransposition intermediate.

name: Ribonucleoprotein particle transport
description: Ribonucleoprotein particle transport is the movement of ribonucleoprotein complexes within the cell.

name: Ribonucleotide Excision Repair (RER)
description: Ribonucleotide Excision Repair (RER) is a pathway that completes the removal of ribonucleoside monophosphates (rNMPs) from DNA after incision by RNase H2.

name: Ribosomal association
description: Ribosomal association is the process by which Alu RNA interacts with ribosomes.

name: Ribosome stalling
description: Ribosome stalling is a condition where the ribosome pauses during translation elongation, which is recognized by the Alu ribonucleoprotein particle.

name: S phase
description: S phase is the stage of the cell cycle when DNA replication occurs and when LINE-1 retrotransposition peaks.

name: S-phase insertion
description: A proposed mechanism for the nonrandom genomic distribution of transposons based on insertion timing during DNA replication.

name: S/G2 phase DNA repair
description: S/G2期DNA修复，指在细胞周期S期和G2期活跃的DNA修复通路。

name: SINE RNA mobilization
description: The process by which non-autonomous SINE RNAs, such as those derived from 7SL RNA or tRNA, hijack the LINE-1 ORF2 protein machinery to achieve their own retrotransposition.

name: SINE activation
description: SINE activation refers to the upregulation of SINE transcript levels and retrotransposition following cellular stress.

name: SINE induction
description: SINE induction is the transcriptional upregulation of SINE elements triggered by specific stimuli.

name: SINE retrotransposition
description: SINE retrotransposition is the process by which SINE elements move within the genome through an RNA intermediate.

name: SINE retrotransposition assay
description: An experimental method developed to monitor the activity and insertion of SINE retrotransposons in cell culture.

name: SINE transcription
description: SINE transcription by RNA polymerase III is a process required for retrotransposition.

name: SINE transcription induction
description: The dramatic increase in the transcription of SINE elements, observed in response to DNA-damaging agents.

name: SINE-SINE recombination
description: SINE-SINE recombination refers to chromosomal rearrangements caused by homologous recombination between SINE elements, potentially stimulated by DNA breaks at integration sites.

name: SINE元件移动
description: 某些剪接后的LINE-1 mRNA可能有助于表达ORF2相关产物，从而影响SINE元件的移动性。

name: SRP9/14 binding
description: SRP9/14 binding refers to the interaction between the Alu RNA and the SRP9/14 protein heterodimer.

name: SRP9/14结合
description: Alu RNA与信号识别颗粒蛋白SRP9和SRP14结合，这是其定位到核糖体并启动逆转录转座的关键步骤。

name: SRPK2/SRSF1/VEGFA165 axis activation
description: The molecular mechanism involving the activation of serine/arginine protein kinase 2 (SRPK2), leading to the activation of serine/arginine splicing factor 1 (SRSF1), which subsequently increases the splicing and expression of the VEGF isoform A165 (VEGFA165).

name: STAT1磷酸化
description: 信号转导和转录激活因子1的磷酸化，是其激活并调控下游基因表达的关键步骤。

name: STING-mediated ORF1p degradation
description: STING interacts with LINE-1 ORF1p, sorts it to lysosomes via the ERGIC and Golgi, leading to its degradation.

name: SUMO化
description: 一种控制蛋白质-蛋白质相互作用、亚细胞定位和其他翻译后修饰的修饰过程。

name: SVA integration
description: SVA integration refers to the insertion of SVA retrotransposons into new genomic locations, potentially causing structural variants.

name: SVA trans-mobilization
description: SVA trans-mobilization is the process by which noncoding SVA elements are mobilized in trans by the protein machinery encoded by autonomous LINE-1 retrotransposons.

name: SVA-mediated 3' flanking sequence transduction
description: SVA-mediated 3' flanking sequence transduction is the process by which SVA elements duplicate and mobilize 3' flanking genomic sequences during retrotransposition.

name: SVA反式动员
description: 非自主性SVA元件在反式（trans）中被LINE-1编码的蛋白质机器动员的过程。

name: Seamless genome editing
description: Seamless genome editing is a technique for introducing precise genetic modifications, such as point mutations, without leaving exogenous DNA sequences like selection markers at the target locus.

name: Second-strand cleavage
description: Second-strand cleavage is the rate-limiting catalytic step in the transposition reaction of Hsmar1.

name: Segmental duplication
description: Segmental duplication is a genomic event involving the copying and insertion of a large DNA segment, often containing genes and repeats.

name: Segmental duplication (SD) mobilization
description: Segmental duplication mobilization is the process by which large, duplicated genomic segments are propagated and rearranged.

name: Segmental genomic duplication
description: A process involving the duplication of large genomic regions, which can confound the identification of unique L1 insertion events.

name: Selection
description: Selection is a process in genetic engineering where cells that have successfully incorporated a desired genetic construct (e.g., a resistance gene) are isolated and propagated.

name: Selective isolation of recent L1 sequences
description: A novel method developed to isolate human L1 sequences that transposed in the relatively recent past, based on features like high CpG content and rare cutter sites.

name: Self-renewal
description: Self-renewal is the ability of a stem cell to divide and produce at least one daughter cell that retains its stem cell properties.

name: Self-renewal maintenance
description: The process by which cells, such as human embryonic stem cells (hESCs), maintain their ability to divide and produce identical daughter cells.

name: Senescence-associated chromatin rewiring
description: Extensive remodeling of 3D chromatin architecture during cellular senescence, including compartment switching and increased chromatin loops.

name: Senescence-associated secretory phenotype
description: Senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) is a pro-inflammatory secretome produced by senescent cells, including factors like IL-6 and IL-8.

name: Sequence amplification
description: Sequence amplification refers to the process of generating multiple copies of a DNA segment within the genome.

name: Sequence diversification
description: Sequence diversification refers to the process of generating genetic variation within a gene family, such as through gene conversion.

name: Sequence homology
description: Sequence homology refers to the similarity in the nucleotide or amino acid sequences of different genes or proteins, suggesting a common evolutionary origin.

name: Sequence transduction
description: Sequence transduction is a process where a mobile element, such as an L1, carries flanking genomic sequences to a new insertion site during retrotransposition.

name: Sequence-specific DNA binding
description: Sequence-specific DNA binding is the ability of a protein to recognize and bind to a particular DNA sequence motif.

name: Sequence-specific insertion
description: A proposed mechanism for the nonrandom genomic distribution of transposons based on insertion site preference.

name: Sequence-specific mRNA degradation
description: Sequence-specific mRNA degradation is the targeted breakdown of messenger RNA molecules, a key outcome of the RNAi mechanism.

name: Sequence-specific single-strand RNA binding
description: Sequence-specific single-strand RNA binding is a specific type of RNA binding where a protein recognizes and binds to particular sequences in single-stranded RNA molecules.

name: Sequestration into stress granules and multivesicular structures
description: Sequestration involves the compartmentalization of LINE-1 products into stress granules and multivesicular bodies, potentially limiting their activity.

name: Sequestration of L1ORF1p
description: Sequestration of L1ORF1p refers to the trapping or confinement of the LINE-1 ORF1 protein within specific cellular compartments.

name: Sequestration of LINE-1 RNP in stress granules
description: Sequestration of LINE-1 RNP in stress granules refers to the confinement of LINE-1 ribonucleoprotein particles within stress granules, limiting their mobility and activity.

name: Shape genomic variation in somatic cells
description: Retrotransposed LINE-1 elements contribute to genomic variation in somatic cells.

name: Shaping the human genome
description: Retrotransposons have played a role in shaping the structure and evolution of the human genome.

name: Signaling modulation
description: SINE ncRNAs can modulate signaling events.

name: Silencing of transposable elements
description: Silencing of transposable elements refers to the complex regulatory mechanisms that suppress the activity of transposons in mammalian cells.

name: Single-strand DNA break induction
description: The process of creating breaks in single-stranded DNA, which is a step in LINE-1 integration.

name: Single-strand hairpin formation
description: Single-strand hairpin formation is a secondary structure that can make introns unstable and facilitate exon deletion.

name: Single-stranded RNA binding
description: Single-stranded RNA binding is a specific activity of the L1ORF1 protein.

name: Single-stranded nucleic acid complex formation
description: The formation of distinct complexes with single-stranded nucleic acids.

name: Site-specific DNA binding
description: Site-specific DNA binding is the ability of a protein to recognize and bind to a specific DNA sequence.

name: Site-specific endonuclease activity
description: Site-specific endonuclease activity refers to the enzymatic function that cleaves DNA at specific nucleotide sequences.

name: Site-specific gene targeting
description: Site-specific gene targeting refers to the precise insertion of a therapeutic gene into a predetermined genomic location.

name: Site-specific insertion of large genetic constructs
description: Site-specific insertion of large genetic constructs is a genome editing goal involving the precise integration of substantial DNA sequences into a targeted genomic location.

name: Site-specific single-nucleotide substitution
description: Site-specific single-nucleotide substitution is a precise genome editing technique aimed at changing a single nucleotide at a defined location in the genome.

name: Skeletal muscle development
description: Skeletal muscle development is the process of formation and maturation of skeletal muscle tissue.

name: Sloping shores
description: A graded influence of a hypomethylated CpG island on the DNA methylation status of flanking CpG sites.

name: Smad4 sequestration
description: Smad4 sequestration is a molecular mechanism where L1ORF1p interacts with Smad4 and retains it in the cytoplasm.

name: Smad4-signaling regulation
description: Smad4-signaling regulation is a pathway involved in tumorigenesis, which L1ORF1p participates in by sequestering Smad4.

name: Small interfering RNAs (siRNAs)
description: Small interfering RNAs are short RNA molecules that can trigger RNA interference to suppress gene expression.

name: Soma to germline inheritance of extrachromosomal genetic information
description: A proposed model where genetic information from somatic cells can be transferred to and inherited via germ cells (sperm).

name: Somatic DNA alteration
description: Somatic DNA alteration refers to changes in DNA nucleotide sequences that occur in somatic cells, distinct from the germline.

name: Somatic L1 insertion
description: Somatic L1 insertion refers to the integration event of a LINE-1 element into the genome of a somatic cell.

name: Somatic SVA retrotransposition
description: Somatic SVA retrotransposition refers to SVA insertion events occurring in somatic cells, potentially during different tissues or developmental stages.

name: Somatic TE insertion
description: Somatic TE insertion refers to a transposable element insertion that occurs in somatic cells and is not inherited.

name: Somatic and germ-line mosaicism
description: Somatic and germ-line mosaicism refers to the presence of genetically distinct cell populations within an individual, arising from a mutation during early embryonic development.

name: Somatic copy number variant (CNV)
description: Somatic copy number variant is a change in the number of copies of a specific genomic region that occurs in somatic cells.

name: Somatic deletion
description: Somatic deletion is a type of structural variation where a segment of DNA is lost in somatic cells.

name: Somatic genome alteration
description: Somatic genome alteration refers to changes in the DNA sequence of somatic cells, which can affect cell physiology and contribute to diseases like cancer.

name: Somatic genome mosaicism
description: Somatic genome mosaicism refers to the presence of genetically distinct cell populations within an individual, which can be caused by somatic retrotransposition events.

name: Somatic genomic mosaicism
description: Somatic genomic mosaicism refers to the presence of genetically distinct cell populations within an individual, arising from post-zygotic mutations such as retrotransposition events.

name: Somatic insertions
description: Somatic insertions refer to the integration of transposable elements into the genome of somatic cells.

name: Somatic mutagenesis
description: Somatic mutagenesis refers to the generation of mutations in somatic cells, which can be caused by LINE-1 retrotransposition.

name: Somatic retroinsertion
description: Somatic retroinsertion refers to the insertion of retrotransposon sequences into the genome of somatic cells, contributing to genomic mosaicism.

name: Somatic retrotransposon insertion
description: Somatic retrotransposon insertion refers to the acquisition of new mobile element insertions in somatic cells, contributing to genetic variation.

name: Somatic transposition
description: Somatic transposition refers to the movement of transposable elements within the genome of somatic cells, often observed in tumors.

name: Source-tracing
description: Source-tracing is a strategy to identify the specific genomic source element responsible for a new insertion event.

name: Sox2介导的调控
description: Sox2表达下调与L1转录和逆转录转座活性增加相关。

name: Sp1-dependent gene expression
description: Sp1-dependent gene expression refers to transcriptional activation driven by the host transcription factor Sp1.

name: Sp1依赖性基因表达
description: 由宿主转录因子Sp1驱动的基因转录过程。

name: Speciation
description: Speciation is the evolutionary process by which populations evolve to become distinct species.

name: Species-specific changes in gene expression
description: Alterations in gene expression patterns that are unique to a particular species, which can be associated with species-specific transposable element insertions.

name: Species-specific differential methylation
description: Species-specific differential methylation refers to differences in DNA methylation patterns between species, often associated with sequence changes.

name: Species-specific domain boundary introduction
description: The evolutionary process where L1 insertions create domain boundaries unique to a species.

name: Species-specific regulatory phenotypes
description: Regulatory characteristics, such as gene expression patterns, that are unique to a particular species lineage.

name: Splicing alteration
description: Splicing alteration is a change in the normal pattern of pre-mRNA splicing, which can lead to aberrant gene products.

name: Splicing disorder
description: Splicing disorder refers to a disease state caused by abnormal RNA splicing, often due to mutations in splice sites or regulatory elements.

name: Splicing regulation
description: Splicing regulation refers to the control of the process by which introns are removed from pre-mRNA and exons are joined together.

name: Splicing suppression
description: Splicing suppression refers to the cellular mechanisms that prevent the inclusion of transposable element sequences into mature mRNA transcripts.

name: Spontaneous insertion
description: Spontaneous insertion refers to the de novo integration of a transposon into the genome, which can occur in germ cells, somatic tissues, and cancer cells.

name: Stabilizing p120-catenin
description: The process of increasing the stability and preventing the degradation of the p120-catenin protein.

name: Stable coexistence
description: Stable coexistence describes a state where autonomous and nonautonomous retrotransposons can persist together in a genome over long periods.

name: Stable gene silencing
description: Stable gene silencing refers to the long-term suppression of gene expression.

name: Stable genomic integration
description: Stable genomic integration is the process by which a DNA sequence, such as a retrotransposed element, is permanently inserted into the host genome.

name: Stable integration
description: Stable integration refers to the permanent insertion of a transgene into the host chromosome, allowing for long-term maintenance.

name: Stable protein expression
description: Stable protein expression refers to the sustained production of a protein in a cell line over multiple generations.

name: Stable transfection
description: Stable transfection is a process that results in the long-term integration and expression of foreign genetic material into the host cell genome.

name: Stable transgene expression
description: Stable transgene expression refers to the long-term, sustained expression of an introduced gene in a cell line or organism.

name: Staggered DNA double-strand break repair
description: Staggered DNA double-strand break repair is a DNA repair pathway that may be involved in the integration of retrotransposons via alternative mechanisms.

name: Staggered nicking
description: A mechanism involving two nicks at specific, non-random sequences in the target DNA to facilitate integration.

name: Stimulation of innate immunity
description: Retrotransposon expression can stimulate cellular innate immune responses through endogenous nucleic acid patterns.

name: Strand asymmetry
description: Strand asymmetry refers to the unequal distribution of transposable elements, such as LINE-1, between the two DNA strands of a genomic region.

name: Stress granule assembly
description: Stress granule assembly is a cellular process where RNA-protein complexes aggregate in response to various stresses.

name: Stress granule localization
description: The process by which the LINE-1 ORF1 protein localizes to cytoplasmic stress granules.

name: Stress response
description: Stress response is a cellular process activated under various stress conditions to maintain homeostasis.

name: Stress-induced L1 reactivation
description: Stress-induced L1 reactivation refers to the process where cellular stress leads to the increased activity of LINE-1 elements.

name: Stringent-type retrotransposition
description: A type of LINE retrotransposition characterized by the recognition of the 3' tail of its own RNA by reverse transcriptase (RT).

name: Structural element in genome
description: LINE-1 elements can function as structural components within the genome.

name: Structural rearrangement
description: Structural rearrangement refers to changes in the structure of chromosomes, such as deletions, duplications, inversions, or translocations.

name: Structural variant formation
description: Structural variant formation refers to the processes that generate large-scale genomic rearrangements such as deletions, duplications, insertions, and inversions.

name: Structural variant generation
description: Structural variant generation refers to the creation of genomic rearrangements, such as insertions, deletions, or duplications.

name: Structural variation
description: Structural variation refers to large-scale differences in the genomic architecture of individuals, such as deletions, duplications, and inversions.

name: Structural variation generation
description: Structural variation generation is the process leading to genomic rearrangements such as insertions, deletions, and duplications.

name: Structure-dependent endonuclease activity
description: Structure-dependent endonuclease activity refers to the enzymatic function of ORF2p that recognizes and cleaves DNA based on specific structural features, such as forked or flap structures.

name: Sub-cellular localization
description: Sub-cellular localization is the specific location of a protein or other molecule within a cell.

name: Subcellular RNA localization
description: The specific distribution and targeting of retrotransposon RNA to distinct compartments within the cell, such as cytoplasmic foci or nuclear aggregates.

name: Subcellular distribution
description: The localization of RNA transcripts within specific compartments of the cell, such as the nucleus or cytoplasm.

name: Substrate preference
description: Substrate preference refers to the specificity or selectivity of a protein (e.g., LINE-1 ORF1p) for mobilizing one type of non-autonomous element over another.

name: Sumoylation
description: Sumoylation is a post-translational modification that controls protein-protein interactions and subcellular localization.

name: Super-enhancer assembly
description: Super-enhancer assembly is the formation of large clusters of enhancers that drive high-level expression of key genes.

name: Suppression of homologous recombination
description: Suppression of homologous recombination is a process that prevents the exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes.

name: Suppression of primary cilia formation
description: Suppression of primary cilia formation is the inhibition of the assembly of non-motile sensory organelles that project from the surface of most vertebrate cells.

name: Suppressive mechanisms
description: Suppressive mechanisms are cellular processes that generally inhibit LINE-1 retrotransposition.

name: Suppressor gene activation
description: A process that increases the activity of genes that inhibit cancer development.

name: Sustained expression
description: Sustained expression refers to the prolonged and stable production of a gene product, such as a prime-editor, within a cell.

name: Synaptic complex formation
description: Synaptic complex formation is the process of assembling a protein-DNA complex that brings together transposon ends for catalysis.

name: Syncytin function
description: Syncytin function refers to the role of syncytin proteins, encoded by certain HERVs, in placental development and cell fusion.

name: Syncytiotrophoblast formation
description: Syncytiotrophoblast formation is the process of creating the outer layer of the placenta, essential for nutrient and gas exchange.

name: Syncytium formation
description: The process of cell fusion to form a multinucleated structure, specifically in placental trophoblasts.

name: S期逆转录转座
description: LINE-1逆转录转座在细胞周期的S期达到峰值。

name: T cell activation
description: T cell activation is the process by which T cells are stimulated to proliferate and differentiate into effector cells.

name: T cell engineering
